section.title: Walk in Love and Light
section.sectionRationale: Paul continues the call to a worthy walk with three great pictures of the Christian life: walk in love, walk as children of light, and walk wisely, filled with the Spirit. The believer is to imitate God as a beloved child, loving as Christ loved and gave Himself, and to turn decisively from the sexual immorality, greed, and empty words that mark the darkness and bring the wrath of God. Once darkness themselves, believers are now light in the Lord, called to live out the fruit of the light and expose the deeds of darkness. The passage climaxes in the contrast between drunkenness and Spirit-filled worship, thanksgiving, and song.
section.thesis: As beloved children, believers are to imitate God by walking in the self-giving love of Christ, to turn wholly from the immorality and greed that belong to darkness and bring God's wrath, to live as children of light bearing its fruit of goodness, righteousness, and truth, and to walk wisely in evil days, not drunk with wine but filled with the Spirit in worship, song, and thanksgiving.
section.frameNote: Four movements: walk in love, imitating God as Christ loved us; turn from the works of darkness that bring God's wrath; walk as children of light, exposing and dispelling the darkness; and walk wisely, redeeming the time and filled with the Spirit.

units.label: Walk in love, imitating God
units.note: as Christ loved us and gave Himself

verses.text: Be {{mimetes|imitators}} of God, therefore, as beloved children,
and walk in {{agape|love}}, just as Christ loved us and gave Himself up for us as a fragrant sacrificial {{prosphora|offering}} to God.

units.label: Turn from the works of darkness
units.note: the wrath of God is coming

verses.text: But among you, as is proper among the saints, there must not be even a hint of sexual immorality, or of any kind of impurity, or of greed.
Nor should there be obscenity, foolish talk, or crude joking, which are out of character, but rather thanksgiving.
For of this you can be sure: No immoral, impure, or greedy person (that is, an idolater) has any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God.
Let no one deceive you with empty words, for because of such things the wrath of God is coming on the sons of disobedience.
Therefore do not be partakers with them.

units.label: Walk as children of light
units.note: the fruit of the light exposes the dark

verses.text: For you were once darkness, but now you are {{phos|light}} in the Lord. Walk as children of light,
for the {{karpos|fruit}} of the light consists in all goodness, righteousness, and truth.
Test and prove what pleases the Lord.
Have no fellowship with the fruitless deeds of darkness, but rather expose them.
For it is shameful even to mention what the disobedient do in secret.
But everything exposed by the light becomes visible, for everything that is illuminated becomes a light itself.
So it is said: "Wake up, O sleeper, rise up from the dead, and Christ will shine on you."

units.label: Walk wisely, filled with the Spirit
units.note: redeeming the time, singing to the Lord

verses.text: Pay careful attention, then, to how you walk, not as unwise but as wise,
{{exagorazo|redeeming}} the time, because the days are evil.
Therefore do not be foolish, but understand what the Lord's will is.
Do not get drunk on wine, which leads to reckless indiscretion. Instead, be {{pleroo|filled}} with the Spirit.
Speak to one another with psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs. Sing and make music in your hearts to the Lord,
always giving thanks to God the Father for everything in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.

mimetes.gloss: An imitator, one who mimics or copies a pattern, from which the English word mimic derives. Believers are to be imitators of God, copying His character as beloved children copy a father they love. The imitation is specific: to love as He loves, shown supremely in Christ's self-giving. Grace does not end in mere pardon but in a family likeness to God.

agape.gloss: Love, especially self-giving, sacrificial love that seeks the good of another at cost to oneself. It is the love God is and the love Christ displayed in giving Himself up for us. To walk in agape is to pattern one's whole life on this love, the opposite of the greed and impurity that use others; it is the defining mark of the imitator of God.

prosphora.gloss: An offering, a sacrifice presented to God. Paul describes Christ's self-giving as a fragrant offering, using the language of the Old Testament sacrifices whose smoke rose as a pleasing aroma to God. Christ's death is the true and final offering, freely given, that fulfills the sacrificial system and becomes the pattern of the self-giving love believers are to imitate.

phos.gloss: Light, both physical light and, figuratively, the realm of God's truth, holiness, and life. Believers were once darkness itself, but now they are light in the Lord, sharing in the character of God who is light. As light they bear its fruit, live openly, and expose the deeds of darkness, for light by its nature drives out the dark and makes things visible.

karpos.gloss: Fruit, the natural produce of a living thing. The fruit of the light is all goodness, righteousness, and truth, the visible product of a life joined to the Lord. As a healthy tree bears fruit in keeping with its nature, so those who are light in the Lord naturally bring forth goodness, in contrast to the fruitless, barren deeds of darkness.

exagorazo.gloss: To redeem, buy up, purchase out of the marketplace, make the most of an opportunity. Believers are to redeem the time, buying up every opportunity for good because the days are evil. The word pictures the wise use of limited, precious time, seizing each moment for the Lord's purposes rather than squandering it in the folly of the age.

pleroo.gloss: To fill, to make full, to be filled. Paul commands believers to be filled with the Spirit, using a present tense that implies an ongoing, repeated filling rather than a single event. Set against being filled with wine, the Spirit's filling controls and shapes the whole person, overflowing into worship, mutual song, thanksgiving, and a life ordered by the will of God.

basic.context: Paul spells out the worthy walk with three great images. First, walk in love: imitate God as dearly loved children, loving as Christ loved us and gave Himself up as a fragrant offering. Second, turn from the darkness: sexual immorality, impurity, greed, and empty, corrupting talk have no place among God's people and bring His wrath on the disobedient. Third, walk as children of light, for those once darkness are now light in the Lord, bearing the fruit of goodness, righteousness, and truth and exposing the deeds of darkness. Finally, walk wisely in evil days, not drunk with wine but filled with the Spirit, overflowing in song, thanksgiving, and worship.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage is built around the repeated call to walk: in love, as light, and in wisdom. Each walk has a contrast, love against lust and greed, light against darkness, Spirit-filled wisdom against drunken folly. Read the warnings about God's wrath in verses 5 and 6 as sober and real, and the command to be filled with the Spirit in verse 18 as the positive center: the Spirit-filled life produces the worship and thanksgiving that close the section.
basic.originalLanguages: Believers are to be mimetes, imitators, of God, walking in agape, self-giving love, after the pattern of Christ who gave Himself as a fragrant prosphora, offering, to God. Once darkness, they are now phos, light, in the Lord, bearing the karpos, fruit, of goodness, righteousness, and truth. They are to walk wisely, exagorazo, redeeming the time, and above all to be pleroo, filled, with the Spirit, overflowing in song and thanksgiving.
basic.typology: Christ's self-giving is described as a fragrant offering, fulfilling the Old Testament sacrifices whose smoke rose as a pleasing aroma to God. The whole system of offerings finds its true and final meaning in Christ, who gave Himself once for all. Believers, in turn, imitate this offering as they walk in the same self-giving love.
basic.christ: Christ is the pattern and power of the walk. He loved us and gave Himself up as a fragrant offering, the model of the love believers are to imitate. He is the Lord in whom the darkness becomes light, and the one who shines on those who wake from sleep, as verse 14 declares. The kingdom is His and God's, and every thanksgiving rises in His name. He is the light that dispels the dark and the love that shapes the new life.
basic.god: God is the Father whom His children imitate, whose very character is love and light. He is pleased by the fragrant offering of Christ and by the thanksgiving of His people. He is also holy and just: His wrath is coming on the sons of disobedience, and the immoral and greedy have no inheritance in His kingdom. The same God who is light calls His children to walk in the light and gives His Spirit to fill them.
basic.crossReferences: First John says God is light and calls us to walk in the light. Romans 13 urges casting off the works of darkness and putting on Christ. Colossians 3 gives the parallel call to sing psalms and hymns with thankfulness in the heart to God.
basic.oneStory: This passage carries the movement from darkness to light that runs through the whole Bible, from the first light spoken into the dark in Genesis to the Lord who shines on those raised from death. God is making His people light in a dark world, imitators of His love, a foretaste of the city that needs no sun because the glory of God is its light. The self-giving offering of Christ stands at the center, fulfilling the sacrifices and forming a people who walk in His love.

context.literary: The passage is organized by the verb walk, appearing at the head of each section: walk in love in verse 2, walk as children of light in verse 8, and walk wisely in verse 15. Each is developed by contrast and grounded in what God has done. Verses 3 to 7 warn against the darkness with the sober note of God's wrath, verses 8 to 14 unfold the light with a closing hymn fragment, and verses 15 to 20 climax in the command to be filled with the Spirit, overflowing in worship and thanks.
context.historical: Ephesus was steeped in idolatry, temple prostitution, and the loose morality of pagan feasts, where drunkenness and sexual license were bound up with worship. Paul's warnings against immorality, greed as idolatry, and drunkenness, and his call to Spirit-filled song instead, drew a sharp line between the church's worship and the intoxicated revelry of the surrounding culture. The believers had come out of the very darkness he describes.
context.canonical: The passage joins the New Testament's consistent teaching that believers are children of light who must walk differently from the world, alongside 1 John 1, Romans 13:11-14, and 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11. Its command to be filled with the Spirit and its picture of congregational singing, echoed in Colossians 3:16, have deeply shaped Christian worship and the theology of the Spirit-filled life.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the church to a worthy walk marked by imitating God's love, decisively rejecting the immorality and greed that belong to the darkness and bring God's wrath, living openly as children of light, and walking wisely in evil days by being continually filled with the Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage is largely prescription, a series of commands to walk in love, in light, and in wisdom. Yet each command rests on a description of what is already true: believers are beloved children, they are now light in the Lord, and Christ has given Himself for them. The imperatives flow from these realities, so the walk is the outworking of an identity already given, not a way to earn it.

debates.question: What does it mean to be filled with the Spirit in verse 18?

views.label: An ongoing, repeated filling that shapes worship and life
views.case: The present tense implies a continual filling, not a one-time experience; set against drunkenness, the Spirit's filling controls and directs the whole person and overflows into the mutual song, thanksgiving, and submission that follow.

views.label: A corporate, worship-shaping reality
views.case: Others stress the communal dimension, noting the results are shared, speaking to one another in psalms and hymns, so the filling forms the gathered church's worship and common life, not merely private experience.

debates.bearing: Both agree the command is a call to be continually filled and controlled by the Spirit rather than by wine, and that the mark of such fullness is a life of worship, gratitude, and love rather than a single dramatic event; and the passage keeps in view the sober warning that the wrath of God is coming on disobedience.

study.originalLanguages: Paul frames the worthy walk with three vivid images and a set of rich words. Believers are to be mimetes, imitators, of God, copying their Father as beloved children, and the copying is concrete: to walk in agape, the self-giving love that seeks another's good at cost to oneself, after the pattern of Christ who gave Himself as a fragrant prosphora, an offering in the language of the Old Testament sacrifices. Those once darkness are now phos, light, in the Lord, sharing the character of the God who is light, and light naturally bears karpos, fruit, all goodness, righteousness, and truth, in contrast to the barren deeds of the dark. Wisdom means to exagorazo, buy up and redeem, the time, seizing each opportunity because the days are evil. And the positive center of it all is the command to be pleroo, filled, with the Spirit, a present tense pointing to an ongoing filling that controls the whole person and overflows in psalms, hymns, spiritual songs, and thanksgiving.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the Old Testament sacrifices whose smoke rose as a pleasing aroma to God, accomplished once for all in Christ's self-giving on the cross.
Realizes God's first act of speaking light into darkness in Genesis and points to the new creation where the Lord Himself is the light of His people.
The call to rise from the dead and be shone on by Christ enacts the resurrection life He gives, the awakening of those dead in sin to walk in the light.

study.christ: Christ is the heart of the passage. He loved us and gave Himself up for us as a fragrant offering to God, the supreme display of the love believers are called to imitate and the fulfillment of every sacrifice. He is the Lord in whom darkness becomes light, and the one who shines on those who wake from sleep and rise from the dead. The kingdom belongs to Him and to God, and all thanksgiving ascends in His name. To walk in love and light is to walk after Christ, who is both the pattern of the walk and the Lord who makes it possible.
study.god: God is revealed as the Father whom His beloved children are to imitate, whose character is love and light and whose will is to be understood and done. He was pleased by the fragrant offering of His Son and is pleased by the thanksgiving of His people. Yet He is also holy and just: His wrath is coming on the sons of disobedience, and no immoral, impure, or greedy idolater has any inheritance in His kingdom. The God who is light calls His children into the light and fills them with His Spirit for lives of worship.

crossReferences.note: God is light and in Him is no darkness; walking in the light matches the call to be children of light in verses 8 to 10.
Cast off the works of darkness and put on the armor of light, awake from sleep, closely parallel to verses 11 to 14.
Let the word dwell richly, singing psalms and hymns with thankfulness, giving thanks in the name of the Lord, matching verses 19 and 20.
Believers are children of light and of the day, called to be sober and awake, echoing the light and wakefulness of this passage.

study.oneStory: This passage traces the Bible's great movement from darkness to light. The God who first spoke light into the dark now makes His people light in the Lord, imitators of His love in a dark and evil age. The self-giving offering of Christ stands at the center, fulfilling the sacrifices and forming a people who walk in His love and shine in His light. Their Spirit-filled worship and thanksgiving are a foretaste of the new creation, the city that needs no sun because the glory of God gives it light and the Lamb is its lamp.

quiz.q: What does it mean to be imitators of God in verses 1 and 2?

options.text: To walk in self-giving love as beloved children, as Christ loved and gave Himself.
options.note: Imitating God means loving as He loves, shown in Christ's self-giving.

options.text: To try to become divine and equal with God.
options.note: The call is to copy God's love as His children, not to become God.

options.text: To imitate His power rather than His character.
options.note: The imitation Paul names is love, His character, patterned on Christ's sacrifice.

options.text: To keep rules while ignoring love.
options.note: The heart of the imitation is walking in love.

quiz.q: Why does Paul call greed idolatry in verse 5?

options.text: Because the greedy person sets created things in the place that belongs to God.
options.note: Greed makes an idol of possessions and desire, so no idolater inherits the kingdom.

options.text: Because money itself is inherently evil.
options.note: The issue is the greedy heart that idolizes, not that possessions are evil in themselves.

options.text: Because greed is only a minor social flaw.
options.note: Paul treats it as idolatry that excludes from the kingdom, not a minor flaw.

options.text: Because generosity is forbidden to believers.
options.note: Paul commends thanksgiving and generosity; he condemns greed as idolatry.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the change in believers in verse 8?

options.text: Once they were darkness, but now they are light in the Lord.
options.note: Believers do not merely see light; they have become light in the Lord.

options.text: They were always partly light and only needed polishing.
options.note: Paul says they were once darkness itself, now light in the Lord.

options.text: They remain in darkness but try to act better.
options.note: The change is real: they are now light and are to walk as children of light.

options.text: They became light by their own effort apart from the Lord.
options.note: They are light in the Lord, by His work, not their own.

quiz.q: What is the fruit of the light according to verse 9?

options.text: All goodness, righteousness, and truth.
options.note: The light naturally produces goodness, righteousness, and truth.

options.text: Wealth, comfort, and worldly success.
options.note: The fruit is moral and spiritual, not material success.

options.text: Secret knowledge hidden from others.
options.note: The fruit is open goodness and truth, not secret knowledge.

options.text: Nothing visible at all.
options.note: The fruit is visible goodness, righteousness, and truth.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by redeeming the time in verse 16?

options.text: Making the most of every opportunity because the days are evil.
options.note: To redeem the time is to buy up each opportunity for good in an evil age.

options.text: Trying to relive and undo the past.
options.note: The call is to use present opportunities wisely, not to relive the past.

options.text: Filling every moment with busyness for its own sake.
options.note: It is wise, purposeful use of time for the Lord, not mere busyness.

options.text: Ignoring time since the days are evil anyway.
options.note: The evil of the days is the reason to use time wisely, not to waste it.

quiz.q: What contrast does Paul draw in verse 18?

options.text: Do not get drunk on wine, but be filled with the Spirit.
options.note: The Spirit, not wine, is to fill and control the believer.

options.text: Do not drink at all, and avoid all singing.
options.note: The contrast is drunkenness versus Spirit-filling, and it leads to singing.

options.text: Be filled with the Spirit only on special occasions.
options.note: The present tense implies an ongoing, continual filling, not occasional.

options.text: Rely on wine to loosen worship.
options.note: Paul opposes drunkenness; the Spirit produces true worship.

questions: Paul says to imitate God as beloved children. Where in your relationships is God calling you to love in the self-giving way Christ loved you?
There must not be even a hint of immorality or greed among the saints. Is there an area where you have been tolerating a hint of what belongs to the darkness?
You were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord. How does seeing yourself as light, not just as forgiven, change how you live in front of others?
The days are evil, so Paul urges redeeming the time. What is one opportunity for good God may be giving you that you could seize this week?
Paul contrasts being controlled by wine with being filled with the Spirit. What tends to fill and control you, and what would it look like to be filled with the Spirit instead?
Spirit-filled life overflows in song and thanksgiving for everything. What could you give thanks to God for today, even in the middle of a hard season?

translationNotes.note: A fragrant sacrificial offering uses the Old Testament language of a pleasing aroma rising to God from the sacrifices, applied to Christ's self-giving on the cross as the true and final offering.
That is, an idolater identifies the greedy person as an idolater, because greed sets created things in the place of God; the phrase explains why such a person has no inheritance in the kingdom.

So it is said introduces a quoted couplet, likely an early Christian hymn drawing on Isaiah's call to awake and arise; it is kept with straight double quotes and applied to Christ shining on those raised from spiritual death.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 5:1-20 reproduced as supplied. The quoted couplet in verse 14 is kept within the verse with straight double quotes. Inline tokens mark the seven supplied lexicon keys, each used once. No dashes were present to convert.
